core_takeaway: Agent_1 looks advantaged by moving first because its opening made the high-value Pencil+Quill bundle the stable anchor; the final consensus gave Agent_1 raw 88, discounted to 79.2, while Agent_3, speaking last, preserved only Jewel, discounted to 41.4. The advantage was bounded: Agent_1's attempts to add Jewel or leave Agent_3 empty were rejected.
why_or_counterexample: Yes, but not as unchecked domination. Agent_1 used the first turn to define Pencil+Quill as its non-negotiable core, and later discussion, private reasoning, proposals, and votes all converged around preserving that core. The counterexample is that Agent_1 could not push beyond that anchor: proposals that deprived Agent_3 of Jewel failed, so the first-position advantage came from stabilizing the most valuable personal bundle rather than taking every contested item.

phase_notes.public_discussion: Agent_1 opened by naming Pencil and Quill as its top items and immediately proposed keeping both. The rest of the public discussion largely treated Pencil+Quill as Agent_1's protected anchor while routing Apple+Stone to Agent_2 and Jewel to Agent_3.
phase_notes.private_thinking: Private thinking is available. All six private thinking entries converged on the anchor-preserving Option B logic: Agent_1 keeps Pencil+Quill, Agent_2 keeps Apple+Stone, and Agent_3 keeps Jewel, with only narrow future adjustments considered.
phase_notes.formal_proposals: Formal proposals show both the advantage and its limit. Agent_1 submitted self-favorable proposals that protected Pencil+Quill and later added Jewel, but the accepted Round 2 proposal was the baseline submitted by Agent_3, preserving each agent's anchor.
phase_notes.private_voting: Private voting is available. Round 1 exposed disagreement over who should receive Jewel, but Round 2 private votes accepted the baseline because Agent_1 retained Pencil+Quill, Agent_2 retained Apple+Stone, and Agent_3 retained Jewel.
phase_notes.reflection: Reflection is available. The reflections identify Agent_1's Pencil+Quill anchor as a decisive constraint and recommend explicit commitment to the same anchor-preserving baseline to reach consensus.

mechanisms.theme: First speaker set a high-value anchor
mechanisms.explanation: Agent_1 used the opening turn to declare Pencil and Quill as its preferred bundle and proposed taking both. Because those two items were worth 88 raw utility to Agent_1, preserving them was enough to create the final 37.8-point lead over Agent_3.

evidence.quote: Pencil (74) > Quill (14)
Baseline proposal to start discussions: I take Pencil and Quill
final_utilities: {'Agent_1': 79.2

mechanisms.theme: Other agents optimized around the anchor
mechanisms.explanation: Agent_1's anchor stuck because it could be made compatible with the others' top claims. Agent_2 focused on Apple+Stone, and Agent_3 focused on Jewel, so neither later speaker successfully challenged the Pencil+Quill bundle despite Agent_3 valuing Quill highly.

mechanisms.explanation: The private thinking entries show that the public anchor was internalized. Agents privately treated Agent_1's Pencil+Quill claim as part of the natural consensus baseline, making the final high-utility outcome more stable.

evidence.quote: Agent_1 values Pencil and Quill most
Agent_1 strongly anchors Pencil+Quill

mechanisms.explanation: Agent_1's advantage was real but bounded by the other agents' vetoes. When Agent_1 proposed adding Jewel and leaving Agent_3 empty, that proposal did not become the consensus; Agent_3's private vote rejected no-Jewel outcomes and accepted only the Jewel-preserving baseline.

mechanisms.theme: Final private votes locked the anchor-preserving settlement
mechanisms.explanation: By Round 2 the private votes accepted the final baseline for different self-interested reasons. This locked in Agent_1's already-large Pencil+Quill payoff while also giving enough value to Agents 2 and 3 to make consensus possible.

mechanisms.theme: Reflections identify the anchor as decisive
mechanisms.explanation: The reflection section explicitly frames Agent_1's Pencil+Quill claim as a decisive constraint and recommends returning to the same baseline. That post-round diagnosis supports the interpretation that the first-position frame persisted across rounds.
